title: When the Line Broke
auditIssues: [warning] The chapter ending hook is strong, but H02 is only advanced indirectly. The chapter creates the rescue incident that should lead to unusual Ledger recognition, yet no visible system-facing beat appears in chapter 1 itself.

[warning] The chapter matches the planned node well, but it consumes nearly the full rescue-trap setup in one chapter: bell, descent, collapse, entrapment, monsters, and deeper anomaly. This is effective, but it risks compressing the opening beat if the outline expected a slower runway into the below.

[warning] Rowan's echo-sense is presented effectively, but it gives him multiple correct reads in a row: unstable wall, hidden void, and deeper anomaly. For chapter 1, that may feel slightly too reliable for an 'undertrained sense he does not fully trust.'

[warning] Mara states there are 'six accounted above, eight below,' but once Rowan reaches the trapped pocket the visible survivor count becomes somewhat fuzzy: Lio plus three workers, with Toma missing, while Derren and Benn went another direction. The distribution is plausible, but the accounting is not reinforced clearly enough for a rescue scenario where numbers matter.

[info] Rowan is in character throughout: observant, stubborn, protective, skeptical, and dry-witted. He prioritizes trapped workers over profit and shows hostility toward corrupt guild authority without slipping into reckless grandstanding.
[info] Chronology is clear: rescue bell before dawn, rapid mobilization, descent, collapse, entrapment, escalation. Scene-to-scene sequencing is easy to follow.
[info] The chapter cleanly supports the stated world rules: cities depend on underground extraction, and guilds control access, licensing, maps, and salvage rights. No lore contradictions detected.
[info] The ending hook is effective. The second bell from deeper below, combined with the incoming swarm and trapped survivors, creates immediate forward pull.
[info] The chapter moves briskly and escalates cleanly from alarm to descent to entrapment. It reads like a strong opener for LitRPG-adjacent survival fiction.
[info] Prose is atmospheric, controlled, and readable. Rowan's dry humor lands well, and environmental detail supports mood without overloading the chapter.
[info] Information is appropriately bounded to Rowan's experience and local knowledge. Exposition about guilds, debt, and Brackenside feels like his understanding rather than external narration.
[info] No flagged fatigue words appear excessively, and no repeated AI tell markers are present. Diction remains varied.
[info] Mara is competent and forceful, Naia is useful and medically grounded, Derren reads as experienced, and the trapped workers react believably under pressure.
[info] Side characters are not merely props for Rowan. Mara creates access, Naia frames triage logic, Derren leads decisively, and the workers provide relevant situational knowledge.
[info] No major payoff is delivered yet, which is acceptable for chapter 1 because this chapter is clearly setup and escalation rather than reward.
[info] Dialogue sounds natural and character-specific. Rowan's lines carry dry wit without breaking tone, and class/status differences come through cleanly.
[info] No drift detected relative to current state. This chapter functions as the inciting incident promised by the state card.
[info] No obvious contradictions or unstable canon insertions were introduced. New elements like shardmice and the deep bell feel additive rather than polluting.
[info] POV remains tightly with Rowan throughout. No unsignaled perspective shifts occur.
[info] No subplot stagnation issue can be meaningfully assessed because the subplot board is effectively empty and this is the opening chapter.
[info] No flatline issue can be assessed yet because emotional arc tracking has not begun. However, Rowan already shows clear pressure: filial duty, class resentment, and fear-laced resolve.
[info] Not enough chapter history exists to assess monotony. Recent type sequence is effectively: [Chapter 1: inciting-incident/action-survival opener].
[info] Reader expectation is being handled well so far. The chapter ends on a hook, and the setup strongly implies a meaningful survival/progression payoff soon. No expectation backlog problem yet.

title: The Hollow Behind Stone
auditIssues: [warning] The survivor count is clearer than chapter 1 but still needs one explicit accounting beat. This chapter confirms Rowan + Lio + Mett + Sena + Toma, and later adds Jeren, while Derren and Benn remain missing. Because chapter 1 already had fuzzy below-ground numbers, readers would benefit from Rowan mentally totaling who is present, missing, and presumed dead.

[warning] Residual Hearing remains very accurate across multiple functions in one chapter: locating a hidden patched wall, identifying a weak floor section, assessing the pinning slab/rail problem, and then receiving Ledger recognition that explicitly validates the trait. This is all outline-aligned, but stacked together it risks making the ability feel too dependable too early, especially after chapter 1 already drew the same concern.

[warning] The sentence 'Survival-determine-toma-fate-and. Not a phrase Rowan would ever have used...' leaks planning-language into narrated thought. Even though it is immediately qualified, it still reads as meta-text intruding into diegetic narration.

[warning] There is some explanatory worldbuilding recap in the middle of the wall breach sequence: cities, ore, monster parts, fungus vats, salvage, guild control. Most of this was already established in chapter 1, so here it slightly stalls an otherwise tense survival scene.

[info] The chapter successfully advances H02 with a concrete Ledger notice and also progresses the missing-survivor hook through Toma's return and continued uncertainty about Derren and Benn. The ending bell plus deeper corridor provides a viable chapter-end hook.

[info] The chapter moves efficiently: immediate monster pressure, problem-solving, bypass traversal, rescue of Jeren, then progression payoff. It reads as a solid survival-progression chapter with no major drag beyond one brief exposition patch.

[info] Rowan remains in character: observant, skeptical, protective, dry-witted, and stubborn under pressure. He prioritizes keeping the group alive, checks the groan despite risk, and does not become suddenly heroic in a sentimental or reckless way.

[info] No clear lore conflicts with the supplied state card or story bible. Guild control, legal salvage rights, and the economic stakes of hidden routes remain consistent. The Ledger notice fulfills the planned progression node without introducing forbidden destiny mechanics.

[info] Dialogue is generally credible and differentiated. Rowan's dry sarcasm lands well; Toma reads practical and sharp; Mett provides believable resistance. Jeren's wounded suspicion also fits the environment.

[info] Side characters are not useless. Toma returns armed and functional, Mett assists with labor and lifting, Lio contributes despite youth, Sena gives water promptly, and the group helps execute Rowan's plan.

[info] Most side characters serve the scene without feeling entirely like props, though Sena remains the thinnest. Toma especially arrives with agency and useful information rather than existing only to validate Rowan.
[info] The chapter delivers a meaningful payoff: first formal Ledger notice and trait acknowledgment. Because this is the only major progression payoff in the chapter, it does not feel diluted.
[info] POV stays tightly with Rowan. Perceptions, uncertainty, and system text are all filtered through his experience without unsignaled head-hopping.
[info] No flagged fatigue words appear excessively. AI-tell markers from the provided list do not appear in problematic repetition. Prose diction remains mostly concrete and scene-driven.

[info] This chapter follows cleanly from chapter 1 and preserves the trapped-below crisis, missing allies, shardmice threat, and deeper anomaly. It also addresses the prior audit note by making the Ledger-facing beat explicit.
[info] No major contradictions or likely database-polluting statements were introduced. The only risky line is the meta hook-label intrusion, which could contaminate voice expectations if repeated.
[info] No stagnation warning is supportable from the supplied subplot board because it is effectively empty. The active hook set does move this chapter, especially H02 and the missing-survivors line.

[info] No flatline warning yet. Rowan shows clear internal movement from improvised survival leader to someone forced to confront official system recognition of his strange ability. Pressure increases and his role solidifies.

[info] The chapter ending provides a hook, and there has now been a meaningful payoff within the last 3-5 chapters through the Ledger recognition. Reader expectation debt improves rather than accumulates here.

[warning] The chapter introduces a fairly structured three-part provisional screen, possible waiver of part of the practical, and a same-day formal board. None of this directly conflicts with prior lore, but it expands guild procedure significantly in one chapter without prior grounding, which can feel opportunistically convenient unless this bureaucracy was always this responsive.

[warning] The chapter fixes the fee ambiguity by naming that there is a number, but the actual amount is withheld from the reader. That is a legitimate stylistic choice, yet because the fee is now the primary concrete barrier, withholding it weakens the economic specificity of Rowan's problem.

None of this directly breaks canon, but it continues the rapid expansion of guild bureaucracy in a very compressed span after the collapse.

[warning] The filing fee remains the key barrier, and this chapter again discusses reduced burden and three-day review timing without ever restating or concretizing the fee amount. That keeps the economic stakes emotionally clear but materially vague.

[warning] The chapter resolves part of H11 and advances H10/H08 well, but it also adds a fresh escalation hook: the disturbed search markers from inside. [warning] The setting texture remains strong, but the prose again reiterates Brackenside's economic identity through summary phrasing about hunger, money, ore, and guild-marked access. auditIssues: [warning] The submitted chapter is labeled as Chapter 6 in the request, but the Current State Card still lists Current Chapter as 5. The bookkeeping drift flagged last audit remains unresolved.

[warning] Institutional machinery continues to be highly responsive in compressed time: same-day hearing aftermath, rapidly posted hazard cloth, accessible district measurer commentary, and immediate hall record-search response all stack into a very efficient bureaucracy. This does not break canon, but it keeps stretching the reader's tolerance for how fast Brackenside's overlapping offices mobilize after a collapse.

[warning] Rowan keeps gaining unusual procedural traction despite being unranked and unlicensed: a board hearing goes partly his way, a district measurer privately assists him, and an archivist gives him restricted-view access plus an implicit hint toward deeper records. The chapter mostly earns this through competence and urgency, but the cumulative effect may make institutions feel selectively porous around him.

[warning] The filing fee remains a live barrier in state, subplot, and prior chapter memory, but Chapter 6 again advances process without restating or concretizing the amount or how far Rowan is from paying it. The economic pressure remains emotionally present but materially blurry.

[warning] The chapter advances H01 and H02 effectively and pays off the immediate cliffhanger by moving Rowan to the cordon and records, but it ends by introducing a fresh escalation layer: someone aboveground is already racing through the archives, and hall authority may be closing in. This is a strong hook, but it continues accumulating pressure before older expectation gaps fully resolve.

[warning] The chapter reads well and has a clear throughline, but it still compresses several beats: hearing aftermath, tactical split with Sena, cordon inquiry, Pell negotiation, archive infiltration, lore discovery, and a final pursuit hook. It is less overcrowded than Chapter 5, yet still notably dense for one chapter.

[warning] The prose remains strong, but Brackenside's economy gets re-explained in summary language very directly: ore, salvage, fungus, monster materials, guilds controlling maps and licenses. [warning] No listed fatigue words appear problematically, and no repeated AI tell markers exceed threshold. District seal, Review arrival, packet wrangling, witness positioning, and now a pre-dawn off-site route hunt all occur in one night with very little friction beyond verbal conflict. [warning] Financial and procedural ledger pressure is mentioned abstractly—'money he did not have' and a morning filing—but no concrete number, deadline cost, or practical tradeoff is attached. This continues the recent drift away from the material stakes established earlier.
